good, good, good.

reach back out to you guys.
I think last week around pricing um just for context.
Um I'm new to multifamily.
I was in the self storage industry for about a decade and I, I had a relationship with a web scraping uh provider to get data.
Um And we had a pricing structure that, that made a lot of sense that we had a much different scale um because we were at 1000 properties in self storage.
So, and that was nationwide.
But
what I'd like to do and it's something that is similar to, to the contract that I had in self storage is to have more of a, you know, I think Phil mentioned that we're growing like crazy.
Um We're probably gonna triple or quadruple in size the next three or four years in terms of proper under management.
We're also expanding significantly right now.
We're in four states.
Um But I would say within the next three years, we'll be in 10.
Um And so we'd like to have this data available, not only for our in operations properties, but also to our, um our acquisitions teams and um really standardized.

My, my goal would be to standardize the process of which we're grading competitors and looking at market rates, irrespective of if it's a property that's an underwriting or property that we're, we're actively managing.

Um, so I, I think there are a lot of benefits there but, uh, love to hear, I, I think the pricing structure that I saw last week was a little confusing at, you know, we were, I think, suggestive like basically 45 cents a unit up to 10,000 units, but then it actually went up on a per unit basis to 60 cents a unit.
So, yeah, I can talk you through that.
So as it stands right now, um our typical pricing structure is per unit per month and based on where you guys currently are with your portfolio, you guys are currently at around 5500 units.
Is that correct?
Ok.
So we extended a price of 50 for 5500 units, 60 cents per unit.
But based on the fact that Phil said that you guys are growing exponentially over the course of the next year, both with your lease ups as well as some openings coming in 2026.
Um He had asked about thoughts on a flat price structure.
Um So what I presented at that point was a flat rate of $4500 a month for up to 10,000 units.
So essentially, if you signed on now, it would be $4500 per month flat rate.
And as you grow up to that 10,000 units, the cost is gonna stay the same.
And what that ends up being once you're at the 10,000 units is the 45 cents per unit.
That's where that monetary number came from.
And then we said once you exceed the 10,000 units at your flat rate, it would be 60 cents per unit per month, we then discuss.
He goes, well, what about 11,000 units?
Um So I got a pricing approval for a flat rate of 4950 to grow up to 11,000 units and the price stays the same every single month.
So it's a known cost and then once you guys exceed the 11,000 units, it would be an additional per unit per month.
We're open to suggestions.
So none of that is set in stone.
That was just a, an option that we laid out to him.
So if you have something in mind on your end, we're happy to hear it out and I can bring that to leadership for sure.
Yeah, I mean, um
I I've gone through some, some similar practices again in self storage and I being new to the industry.
It's pretty frustrating that a lot of vendors tend to focus on the unit count because to me, that's especially with the way that, that we're set up, you know, we have a fairly commoditized product.
We're, we're really only focusing on two bed, two bath units.
Um and that will continue to be the strategy.
So, you know, we're, we're probably uh atypical of a lot of operators.
Um but in storage, it was all based on the property count, right?
Because then your data doesn't really matter.
I understand effectively.
What we're trying to do is is to group uh customers in terms of size of properties.

So you can effectively charge them a percentage of revenue or at least directionally close to what, you know, scale looks like from a revenue perspective because, you know, at the end of the day, it's, it's still very nominal expense in a per unit or per property basis.
Um I just don't like that model for pricing structure.

I'd I'd rather have more of like, can you give us, let's say per market structure and just give us all the data in those markets um and have some kind of a pricing plan like where I, I really would like to achieve more of, hey, we're in 10 states.
What's the pricing to deliver us data for 10 states?
Right?
And, and I'm really more interested in, I think the U I is great.
But ultimately, at the end of the day, that's, that's the, the lowest um focus of ours, I just want the data.
Yeah.

So we, we want to have this data in our data warehouse where we can then set up uh Python scripts that get fed into analytics that drive our revenue management strategies and our um our underwriting approaches.
Yeah.
Um

Well, what's, what's funny is uh talking about that pricing model is apartment IQ has been for like five years and the first two years we went with the per property pricing model, but the industry hated it.
So that's why we switched over to the per unit per, you know, per month.
So I'm with you, it made things a lot easier.
Um So yeah, we especially
because you're incentivized to have properties with low unit count.
Right.
Right.

It's, it's kind of annoying.
Right.
Let me ask you this, I guess in terms of so, so like right now we're like, we're at the $4500 a month.
Do you have a number off the top of your head?
That like makes sense.
I think
that just to, just to put in perspective like, um in self storage, the contract that I had now, this is a couple years ago.
So obviously it's, it's gone up since, but, you know, um for nationwide coverage, I was paying $25,000 a year.
Yeah.

Um And so I had a high degree competence in, in their system.

I, I don't know much about the support team.

Like we're a very technological group, we're, we're building out very sophisticated team.

Um We also had lower revenue numbers and than we have here.
So I understand from a budgeting standpoint, um you know, there's, it's a little bit easier to absorb the cost.
But again, I, I really
like to think about, ok, in five years down the road and we're at, let's say 55,000 units.

Like, I mean, I, I would say probably anything above like
$60,000 a year would be starting.
We have the conversations around.
OK.
It probably makes sense for us to find some resources in India to, to outsource this to and um and really build out our own scraping model.

it would probably just be

that makes sense.

yeah.
